title: Validating Nested HTML Tags with Strict Tag Names

statement.markdown: Write a function to validate whether a given string of HTML tags is valid. A string of HTML tags is valid if it consists of well-formed HTML elements, i.e., each opening tag is followed by a corresponding closing tag. The function should return true if the input string is valid, false otherwise. Note that the function should be case-sensitive and assume that the input string only contains alphanumeric characters, spaces, and '<' and '>' characters.
statement.outputFormat: True if the input string is valid, false otherwise
statement.notes: The function should be able to handle nested tags.

constraints.description: The maximum number of characters in the input string

examples.explanation: This is a valid HTML string with nested tags.
This is an invalid HTML string because the closing tag is missing.
This is an invalid HTML string because the closing tag is not properly nested.

hints.text: Use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags.
Iterate over the string and check if each opening tag matches the corresponding closing tag.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The key insight is to use a stack to keep track of the opening tags.
editorial.bruteForce: One approach is to use a regex pattern to match the HTML string, but this would be inefficient.
editorial.betterSolution: Using a stack is a much more efficient approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: Push each opening tag onto the stack and pop the stack when a closing tag is found.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The function is correct because it checks for both well-formedness and proper nesting.
